exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 1: Put in both/either/neither. Use of where necessary.

promptParts.before: It was a good football match.

questions.grammarRule: 'Both' refers to two people/things together and takes a plural noun. Since it was a good match, both teams played well. Examples: 'Both cities were beautiful.' / 'Both windows are open.'

promptParts.before: It wasn't a good football match.

questions.grammarRule: 'Neither' means 'not either' — it rules out both options and takes a singular noun. Since the match was bad, neither team played well. Examples: 'Neither job was very interesting.' / 'Neither of us was hungry.'

promptParts.before: Is your friend English or American?

questions.grammarRule: 'Neither' can stand alone to reject both options in a question. Since she is Australian (not English or American), the answer is 'Neither.' Example: 'Do you want tea or coffee?' — 'Neither, thanks.'

promptParts.before: We went away for two days but the weather wasn't good. It rained on

questions.grammarRule: 'Both' + plural noun describes something true of two things together, with no 'of' needed directly before a plain noun. Since the weather was bad the whole trip, it rained on both days. Example: 'Both windows are open.'

promptParts.before: I bought two newspapers. Which one do you want?

questions.grammarRule: 'Either' means 'one or the other, it doesn't matter which' and can stand alone as a reply. Example: 'Tea or coffee?' — 'Either, I don't mind.'

promptParts.before: I invited Diana and Mike to the party but

questions.grammarRule: Before a pronoun like 'them', either/neither need 'of' ('neither of them'), while 'both' can drop it. Since neither person showed up, the answer is 'neither of them'. Example: 'Neither of us had a watch.'

promptParts.before: Do you go to work by car or by bus?

questions.grammarRule: 'Neither' rejects both options in a question and can stand alone. Since the speaker always walks (not car or bus), the answer is 'Neither.' Example: 'Cinema or theatre?' — 'Neither, I want to stay home.'

promptParts.before: Which jacket do you prefer, this one or that one? I don't like

questions.grammarRule: Here 'of them' is already given, so only the base word is needed: 'either of them' means the speaker dislikes both options equally. Note: since the verb is already negative ('don't like'), 'neither' can't be used too — that would be a double negative. Example: 'I haven't read either of these books.'

promptParts.before: Paula and I didn't know the time because

questions.grammarRule: Before a pronoun like 'us', either/neither need 'of' ('neither of us'), while 'both' can drop it. Since neither person had a watch, the answer is 'neither of us'. Example: 'Neither of my parents is English.'

promptParts.before: Ann has got two sisters and a brother.

questions.grammarRule: 'Both' can go directly before a plain plural noun with no 'of' needed. Example: 'Both sisters are married.' (same as 'Both of Ann's sisters are married.')

promptParts.before: Ann has got two sisters and a brother. I've met her brother but I haven't met

questions.grammarRule: Before a possessive like 'her sisters', either/neither need 'of' ('either of her sisters') — only 'both' can drop the 'of'. Note: since the verb is already negative ('haven't met'), 'neither' can't be used too — that would be a double negative. Example: 'I haven't read either of these books.'
